So, I'll say let's keep version number three, but four had this really good idea. And five, well, I really like how the UI is or the color scheme, so let's pull those in to version number three, but we're sticking with this sort of timeline as our main one. Everything else is treated like scratch paper. It gets thrown out. We continue on three. And by the way, that three can now get multiplied five to 10 times over and the process starts again. So the 10 agents, they're not working on the same project, but that project gets perfectly cloned and copied. All of them work on the same project, but in parallel on clones of that project. And then at each intersection, I check the work. I combine it as needed. and I continue. And this is what Dan Shipper calls the human sandwich. So human sets the framing. AI does the work or it collapses that task so to speak. So instead of me doing it, it does all that for me whatever it can and then the human judges and extends it. Right? So it's kind of like this sandwich approach. And this explains why the human work survives all of this because the bottleneck is no longer how fast you can type or click on things or navigate the various UIs. that portion gets skipped. But the bottleneck now becomes knowing what to ask for, judging the outputs and determining what happens next. So you have to be a good judge of not just what the AI produces, but if it's collecting data, then reacting to that new data in certain ways. And so that kind of gets us to the key mechanism of all of this. It's that cheap competence creates more work. So what that means is that AI makes what used to be human competence of yester year. It makes that cheap. In the past, you might have been very competent at finding whatever data you needed to online. You just knew where to go, how to find it, how to aggregate it in an Excel into an Excel spreadsheet or a SQL database or whatever. You might have been good at creating some little scripts or coding up some software to help you navigate that data. Then you might have been a really good writer kind of describing what that data means based on your findings and your Excel spreadsheet skills. AI collapses all that competence. It makes it cheap. So what used to be kind of scarce competence is made to be very cheap and very available. Writing a pull request is easy now. So is drafting a newsletter or creating a YouTube thumbnail or summarizing a sales call or some piece of software. It used to be scarce. You used to have to have some competence to do that. AI kind of collapses that. So we knew that already. So this isn't headline news. We kind of knew that this was happening. Here's what I think maybe we missed. And again, some of these ideas aren't new. We're just getting a lot more sort of data on them. And we're realizing that no, this is in fact the thing that's going to push the world this way. That's this idea of the Jebans paradox, although it's usually used to talk about certain valuable resources or expensive resources that when they collapse in price, you know, the demand for them goes through the roof. So let's say you use your car to get to work, get to the grocery store, but gas is very expensive. Then you find some new way to make cars use less of it. So you think, okay, now people are going to use less gas because the cars are more efficient. They can drive instead of 5 miles on one gallon, 30 m or 50 mi, whatever, whatever the case is. But what happens is people decide to drive everywhere. They take a road trip. They just go joy driving, whatever, because it's cheaper, it's easier, it's more efficient, demand goes up, consumption goes up. So the basic idea is when something becomes cheaper, people use more of it. With AI, competence got a lot cheaper. Competence for specific things like the output of the things that the AI model was trained on making code and pros and product specs and documentation support tickets whatever. So it got cheaper, people use more of it. So output explodes. So operations people can now write code. Engineers can write product pages. So the first order effect isn't everyone stops working. The first order effect is everyone's productivity goes up. Everybody produces more stuff. Now, the big problem here, of course, is the slopification of everything, right? Because some of these outputs, they're the same. So, if everybody has access to the same outputs, everything becomes kind of AI slop. And this is the defining factor that I think is going to be so important in a new era is that experts become more important. Yes, anyone can create YouTube thumbnails, but somebody with a very sharp design eye and understanding how they work can create better ones. Hopefully, anyone can produce code and create software, but you really need that engineering mind to understand how to build something properly, how to test it to make sure that it's quality. Anyone can draft a newsletter or a book or a blog post, but you really need great writers and editors to make it into something good, something unique. So, what this means is that automation isn't magic dust that you just sprinkle over work to make it disappear. It just becomes the operating system on which you need still maintenance, evals, right? You need to figure out what's good, what isn't. You need the right sets of permissions. What can the AI automate? What it can't? What is it allowed to do? What it isn't? You need the review cues to make sure that the process keeps going. You need instruction files. A lot of my time, not a lot, but a significant amount of time is spent writing very in-depth, very involved instruction files. And at the end of the day, you do still need human ownership. You need somebody to say, "Hey, like I made sure this is good. This is unique. I put my own flavor on it that it's not just slop. The point is AI can automate the middle of the task. We assume is we still decide what tasks matter, what outcomes we're trying to achieve. And at the end, we need to decide which one of the versions is better, if it's exactly the outcome that we were looking for, and then repeat that process again. So now that we're here, what comes next? Humans still control the beginning and the end. So that means that the work that we do, the jobs, they they change. They don't go away, but they they evolve. The amount of money you're going to be getting paid in the past might have been for the stuff that you you did, for the lines that you typed, but moving forward, companies will be paying humans for the decisions that they make, for them supervising what gets done. They need to integrate it into the company. they need to differentiate it from all the other stuff that's produced and at the end of the day they need to take ownership for that stuff. So this means that this idea of a permanent underclass might not exist. This is not what we're heading towards at least when it comes to jobs. We may yet still have a permanent underclass but not for people in and for workers but rather for companies. That's because AI doesn't help every business equally. There are a lot of companies that their moat, how they make their money, is based on this idea that used to be difficult to produce certain things. The competence was very expensive. We've already seen Anthropic publish a few things that were open- source that crashed the stock valuations of certain companies. So if you had some specific set of code that dealt with some legal aspect for example because it was hard to develop that you needed somebody that was competent in writing code. You need somebody that was competent in the legal profession. They could charge a lot of money per seat for certain companies like legal companies to buy that software then charge and per se to have access to that software. With AI that moat is basically gone overnight. But companies that have the right data workflow, these new people that are able to use AI, they might win bigger and bigger shares of the market. And in fact, AI powered companies might expand and wipe out other companies. This is still kind of unclear. We don't know exactly where this is going to be going. I don't tend to know how this is going to play out, but that might be kind of the next thing to watch. How disproportionate will AI be for companies? So, I think the takeaway at the end of the day is that so far it's looking like maybe the whole jobs going away thing was overstated. Again, we're not saying that this is the new future. We know it for sure. We're saying that the hypothesis changed. So, over time, we'll see more and more data that either proves or disproves this. But for the time being, it might be that the jobs are evolving but not going away. And in terms of how AI will affect the companies, the big question here is is it that AI will raise every company's productivity, you know, let's say 1% or 5% or is it that it's going to take the top 5% of companies, raise their productivity 20%, but not necessarily everyone else. The difference between those two scenarios will make a drastic impact on how business is done, on what the stock market looks like. But the big point here is I think for a lot of us our intuitions about what AI is going to do were off in terms of specifically how it's going to affect jobs. Now I think the best advice that I or maybe anyone can give people that are worried about their jobs is this. Use all the new models as they come out and understand where you fit in into that work process. Think of yourself as managing the inputs and the outputs. So on the input side, what are you prompted with? What context do you give it? Like what model do you use? Everything that you can decide on the input side to improve how well the model works and then on the output decide which of the different versions you're going to use. If the job it completed, was it good? Was it not good? The people that figure out how to do those sides of the equation, I think they're going to do very, very well. But just like we all had to learn to type and interact with computers, I think doing this and working with AI agents will just be table stakes. Also, it does seem like different job titles will maybe either go away or at least become a little bit more diffused, a little bit more vague because again the competence is going away. Like you might have been really good at this particular thing and that was your job title. Now, more and more we're going to be doing a lot of different things across job titles and be more like project managers. Maybe that's a better term for it. So, I'll leave you with this. I think you're going to be seeing a lot of headlines saying that AI companies did a full 180 and kind of flipped on what they were saying before. And some of those publications will probably try to paint this as a preipo change of, you know, marketing and PR, etc. I don't think that's a fair claim. And that's not a fair framing because a lot of us that use this technology on a daily basis, we're all noticing a similar shift. Each new iteration of the model is allowing us to automate more. It does more for us. We're more and more amazed at the stuff that it's able to do. It gives us more productivity, more output, but it doesn't reduce the amount of time that we have to work. If this trend continues, and this is important, we have to realize that it kind of negates the two biggest AI risks that we've ever talked about. First of all, if we have to manage it and make sure the outputs are right, then that whole thing about it killing everybody, well, that's probably not going to happen. Not without a human being sitting there and going like, "Okay, let's make a plan for how to kill everybody." And then on the other side going, "Well, I don't know if that's going to work. Let's get some better ideas. Let's uh let's see if we can improve the process. Which by the way, that idea of humans using AI to do something nefarious, that can always happen. Any technology can be abused. But the specific X risk from rogue AI as it in developing self-awareness and then going all Skynet Terminator 2 style. I always say Terminator 2. I've never seen Terminator 1. I I just saw the second one. To me, that's the Terminator movie. I I don't even know what happens in the first one. But the point is, if this pattern holds, then those two big risks go away. There's no X-risk from rogue AI specifically, and there's no AI job apocalypse. There's a massive explosion in work productivity. How we do work changes. The internet changes. Tons of things still get disrupted, but the two biggest fears might be a thing of the past. Let me know what you think about this. Do you agree with this hypothesis? Do you believe what Sam and Daario are saying or do you think it's just marketing for their IPO? Let me know in the comments. If you made this far, thank you so much for watching. My name is Wes Roth. will see you in the next

items.title: Claude, The Pope, and AGI
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.
items.transcriptSummary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.

transcript.text: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. So let's talk about it. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it. So this doesn't seem like a photo op or some brief meeting. This seems a little bit more involved. So, Anthropic is of course one of the major frontier AI labs led by Daario Amade and others and it is in many ways different from the other labs. They do have a lot of self-imposed moral standards. They are trying to solve this AI alignment thing, making sure that the super intelligence that they build is well behaved and does not harm humanity. Anthropic recently had massive problems with the Department of War based on some of the red lines that it was not willing to walk away from. Spying on US citizens and autonomous weapons powered by AI were the crux of the issue. Anthropic refused to budge and Google and OpenAI became the defense contractors and Anthropic was designated a supply chain risk. But they've been scoring a lot of reputational points recently, both against their push back on the Department of War and recently getting Andre Karpathy to join the team who is very well respected in the industry by pretty much everybody both the academics and the researchers and the engineers and the AI enthusiasts. He is highly regarded and this is yet another big victory in terms of whether you want to call it PR or just winning goodwill amongst the people. I mean this is it. The Catholic Church has something like one and a half billion members and the church announced an ethical collaboration with Anthropic. I mean think about that for just a second. This is the world's first AI company to literally receive the blessings of the pope. Did you expect to hear that headline in your lifetime? By the way, really fast, I've been saying this on this channel probably 100 plus times now since we started and that is AI will intersect with every part of everyone's lives. It's not just a coding tool. It's not just for writing emails. We've seen it hit jobs and art and movies and finance and cyber security. And now it's kind of intersecting with one of the biggest religions on the planet. And again, this is just scratching the surface. Okay, so there's kind of two main players here that we're going to be talking about. One is Christopher Ola. So he is an anthropic co-founder. So he's one of the main people at Anthropic and he's one of the most important figures in AI interpretability. So this isn't a sales guy. This isn't an executive. This guy is a researcher. He's working so that we can understand AI and how it thinks, what makes it tick, how it behaves. He said a few things during the ceremony, during the interview, press conference, whatever you want to call it, that are already being wildly wildly misinterpreted. And the other person is, of course, the pope. So he's kind of representing the views of the church. And between the two of them, there's like three major things that I think this is about. The first one is an understanding that AI will displace jobs. So neither one of those people are mincing their words. They're saying this is coming. Their concern is with humanity, with the people, with the global poor. What do we need to do now to start thinking about the transition? On this channel, by the way, we talk about that quite a bit. As AI automation takes over, the demand for human labor will decrease. I don't know what time scale it's going to happen on over a year, 5 years, 10 years, 50 years, but as a lot of people in the AI field have said like this system will get disrupted. this idea of you contributing mental or physical labor in exchange for resources and being able to interact in in society in the economy that's going away or at least it will change. It will be greatly disrupted and we need some sort of a model some sort of a plan for what happens next. So the fact that more and more people are taking this seriously and talking about this is great. Google DeepMind recently hired a chief AGI economist or something like that. basically someone that's going to start looking into how to navigate that transition. The fact that the Vatican is talking about it is good, too. I don't know what their plans are, but the fact that more people are saying it and more people are hearing this message and we'll need to start kind of wrestling with it and trying to at least understand it, I think overall that's a good thing. The second big message that they're talking about is that these AI labs, well, they're not to just be trusted by default, so to speak. As the Pope said, AI is not neutral. The people that are building it, designing it, they're kind of infusing their own ideas into it. It's important that other people participate in the shaping of AI. And the third is kind of talking about what AI is, how it's a black box that we don't fully understand. And we need to be able to detect dangerous patterns as this thing gets better and better and smarter before it can cause realworld harm. And let's start there because I already see the most confusion out there about this and the thing that Christopher Ola was saying in one of the messages. He talked about the AI models having something that appears to us to be something like emotions or introspection or other things that are similar to what humans possess. And this is where a lot of the conversation breaks down again because words can be a little bit vague. They can have a very different meaning to different people. We covered a lot of research on this channel coming out about anthropic about how these models behave. We've read their paper about introspection about Claude and the other AI models have something that can be called introspection. We've read a paper from Enthropic that covers how these models can have functional feelings. But when a lot of people out there in the world when they hear it and don't really understand those concepts, they will kind of scoff at it. They're going to say, "Oh, the people at Anthropic think that Claude has feelings and we need to be nice to it." Kind of making fun of that idea. Here's the thing. Nowhere in those research papers that we've read, to my knowledge do any of the people at Anthropic, no one's saying that these AI models have some sort of a subjective experience. We're not talking about pain or pleasure, happy or sad. No one's saying this is a living thing. I think a more accurate way of saying it is as we scale up these neural networks that are by the way kind of mimicking the human brain a little bit. The neural network in in our head is what we've tried to recreate digitally with these neural networks. And as we scale them up and task them doing more and more complex things and expose them to more data, certain things emerge. And some of these things are very unique and alien and different from how we've evolved and how the human brain works. But some things seemingly are similar. So if you've ever had an experience where you keep thinking about something and you go, why do I keep thinking about that? So you start thinking about why you're thinking about that thing. You're kind of introspecting. You're thinking about your own thinking processes and trying to understand where they're coming from. Well, it seems that Claude does have something different. It's able to introspect. It's able to notice when certain thoughts and ideas are being injected and it's able to kind of catch them and think about them. Now, I'm sure no one taught you to do that. You just notice that ability one day. And the same thing is true for Claude. They didn't train it to be able to do that. This skill or feature emerged at some point. And it's the same thing with these functional feelings that these models have. So it's a way to create a state that helps them better predict what words to use next. And those states can map to certain feelings that we might have. So it's not saying that they're having those feelings or subjective experiences. It's saying that they're able to model those things and have some representation about those things. So that's why they call them kind of functional feelings. So this is where Christopher Ola's words I think are being a little bit misinterpreted. His point is that these AI models, they're not engineered like airplanes or bridges. They're grown on this massive amount of human knowledge and text and speech. As he says, AI models are made from us, from our worlds, and they are far more subtle and odd and beautiful than science fiction prepared us for. So, I think that's an important point to understand because he's not saying Claude has feelings. What he's saying is these things are weird, mysterious, unsettling, and answers about those things and how they're going to affect the world and our stance towards them. It can't just come from engineers, right? If I get on an airplane or into a car, I want all the safety features, right? I want engineers to have the last word where those things are concerned. I I I want engineers and people with that mindset to be in charge of that. They're saying with AI, that's not enough. We need more different perspectives to to fully understand this thing and where it's going. And the second big point about what they're talking about is the massive massive job displacement that will be triggered by AI. They're saying that AI displacement is now a moral crisis. If there's a real possibility of AI displacing massive amounts of human jobs, then supporting those displaced workers becomes a moral imperative of historic proportions. Again, this is somebody that's a co-founder of an AI lab, a billionaire. This is not a a union organizer. This is not a politician. This is somebody that's leading the charge and at the same time warning against it. The Pope's framing also supports this. He's asking if AI replaces work, what sort of replaces the dignity and the social function that our trade, our work has served before. Also, who captures all those productivity gains? Who pays for the transition? Like how do we transition? What happens to families? What happens to young people if stable employment just disappears? And is universal basic income, is that enough? Or is there some other portions of jobs and work that provide meaning and other things that we need? And the third big thing that I think they're talking about is this idea that AI labs right now, as they say, hold too much power. Interestingly, the Pope's argument is not anti-technology. It's more pro-humanity or anti-dehumanization. The pope doesn't make a lot of points that AI can help heal and educate and unite and and provide a lot of good things just like at the same time it can cause a lot of harm, automate warfare, it can divide people, manipulate, etc. He makes the big point that AI is not neutral. The people that are building it, financing it, promoting it, like all those people have an impact and an effect on how this AI acts, behaves, what ideals it supports. Can you imagine if the church comes out as pro- opensource, like they give their full blessing to open source AI? Wouldn't that be interesting? It does seem fitting, doesn't it? Let me know in the comments if you agree with that. Would organizations like the Catholic Church, would they be pro- open-source AI and open- source software? Now, at the same time, they caution that these private companies, these AI companies, a lot of them about to go public, that they hold state level power. If you think about it, a lot of these companies can now affect markets. They can affect war. We've seen that with anthropic, you know, front and center. How well they're able to hold the line against someone like the department of war. They affect education, geopolitics, and there's really just a handful of them at the center of this. So, the pope is saying regulation is necessary, but it's not enough. This is one of the sort of concerns that I personally have and I apologize for the tangent, but there's a lot of people that are just saying we need more regulation, we need more weigh in, we need more this. It's like this idea that if we just have more people yelling their opinions, then somehow everything's going to get better. Just adding more red tape and bureaucracy doesn't necessarily just make everything better. So right now this is the the first day that we got access to a lot of the statements and the publications that they did. I am very happy about how they're talking about thinking about AI job displacement. But this idea of regulation and insight and oversight and stuff like that, it still feels a little bit vague to me. I I hope they flesh it out a little bit better because there's tons of stuff that you can say that sounds really nice and is hard to argue with. You can say everyone should have a choice and everyone should weigh in. It should work for everybody. It should be democratic and and yes, all those things are good, but they're a little bit meaningless. They're a little bit too vague. What exactly are we talking about? The next big point is the fact that work isn't just about income. So having a job isn't just paycheck. It's an identity. It's belonging. It's dignity. It's family stability. By the way, this I think is a very important thing. This is my personal belief that we need to separate, right? This idea of getting resources in exchange for labor from the meaning and dignity and political power because workers do have political power in their ability to refuse to work, shutting down key industries. Certainly, we've seen examples of nations where they have enough natural resources, where they're not as reliant on the population as some other nations. And there does seem to be a pattern, not always, but in some cases, of maybe those governments not being the nicest to their people. So, if you're not afraid of the population refusing to work, you you might overstep your bounds a little bit. Whereas if your nation requires the workforce to work and to play nice with you, you're going to be forced to be nicer. So whatever model we're transitioning to or at least we're thinking about it has to take those things into account, right? So not just the paycheck, the resources needed to survive, family stability, etc. We also need to think about what happens when the powers that be don't need workers cuz we've been replaced with AI and robots. One thing I've always said is I don't think UBI is going to help if it's made like a just a cash payment, like a monthly cash payment, because there's way too many tricks that you can play to mess with people, right? You can take it away if they don't support you, if they don't say the right things, or you can print more money, dilute the money supply so that you know a,000 bucks a month or whatever it is becomes meaningless. Whatever new system we have, it has to be equity based. If you're a unique human being, you have to have some ownership that cannot be ever taken away from you. You also can't take out loans. You can't go in debt against this thing. Like it has to be just protected from anything like you have this equity, this ownership in the nation that you live in or whatever other organization sort of makes sense and it can't be taken away from you and it gives you some share of the profits. One thing that I'm really in favor of is having it somehow be based in compute. That's the currency, the money of modern times. We need compute to run these systems. So if this gets scaled up, everybody loses their jobs. But every single individual has some sort of share of the global compute pie and get some sort of dividends based on how well these robots and AIs are working. Then everyone's incentives are aligned. It's okay if robots do the job and we get a cut of those productivity gains. There is a way to make this work and make it good for everybody. We just have to kind of model it out and figure out what it is. All right. So this is something that I think we need to watch carefully because this is of course a very large powerful influential religion organization. It's a global force. One and a half billion members. Members or followers, whatever word you want to use there. I think how this conversation continues is going to be very important. How the world deals with AI. So far, a lot of it seems to be very positive. We're talking about how to make the transition if and when AI starts displacing jobs. We're beginning to talk about some of the weirder aspects of how we're growing these AI models. And I know there's going to be a lot of push back on it. Sure, some people are going to be framing this as anthropic, trying to prove that Claude has a soul. But the point is that the Vatican is stepping in at the exact moment when these AI labs are beginning to kind of grapple with the concept that these AI models aren't just tools. That there are some humanlike cognitive processes. And of course there's going to be people in the future that will push towards personhood and these things maybe have some moral considerations. And I think Anthropic's point is that it is right now a little bit vague. We don't have proof one way or another. We are just beginning to get insights into this and how the conversation develops is going to be important. So with that said, let me know what you think about this whole thing. Religion has spent centuries building moral constitutions for human behavior. Do you agree that they should also build constitutions for AI, ethical and moral behaviors? Do you think that has a place in conversation? Maybe maybe you don't. Maybe you're you're scared that this is happening and it might influence everything in a negative way. Let me know. I'm very curious to know how people are perceiving this. Make sure you're subscribed. Please hit thumbs up. It helps more than you know. My name is Wes Roth and I will see you in the next

items.title: The REAL Reason Andrej Karpathy Joined Anthropic
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story.
items.transcriptSummary: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story.

transcript.text: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story. Getting Carpathy to join your lab is a big big win for a number of different reasons. He's not just a top tier researcher. He's an icon, if you will. Meaning, legitimately, he's a cultural influence at this point. He can explain hard technical ideas and make it kind of obvious, make it easy to understand. He's also, at least for the time being, shutting down his AI education company. But I think there's a whole other story developing here that people are missing. And it has to do with what Andre has been working on in his time away from OpenAI. And this same thing also seems like it's causing a pretty big rift in opinions from the heads of these major AI labs with Google on one side and Anthropic on the other. So, first and foremost, what actually happened? Andre Karpathy announced that he's joining Anthropic on May 19th, 2026. His new role is to be part of Anthropic's pre-training team. He reports to Nick Joseph, Anthropic's head of pre-training. Anthropic told multiple outlets exactly what Karpathy will be working on. He will build a new team focused on, this is the important part, using Claude to accelerate pre-training research. And just on the off chance, you don't know Claude isn't just a guy, it's the AI. I didn't mean for that to rhyme. What I'm saying is Claude is anthropics AI. Karpathy will be using AI to accelerate pre-training research. So he's going to use AI to improve future versions of AI. Since Andre left OpenAI, he's been releasing a number of opensource projects in the AI space. They were very interesting. But if I had to pick one that really stands out, it's Auto Researcher. Auto Researcher uses a very small large language model to do machine learning research. It's kind of those kits that you get kids for them to start learning engineering skills. You know what I mean? Like you you actually use electricity or whatever physics or whatever you're trying to get them to learn about, but it's kind of like scaled down and simplified. That's kind of what auto researcher is. It allows all of us to start dipping our feet into doing automated machine learning research. Now, it's a lot bigger than that because as Karpathy himself said it, a lot of these little wins that you can get from your home computer, from these small language models running on your home computer, those can translate and scale up to actual training for actual large language models. I am like 99% sure I put out a video about the auto researcher, but in case you haven't heard of it, Fortune magazine actually described it in a very interesting way, in a very simple to understand way. They called it the Carpathy loop and that phrasing is actually very very useful because it captures that recursive structure. How it works is AI proposes a change. items.title: OpenAI just SOLVED MATH....
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So 7 months ago, OpenAI claimed that one of their models solved a problem that no human or AI has ever solved before. This ended up being a great embarrassment for OpenAI.

items.title: GPT 5.6 vs Mythos 5 LEAKS, It's Not Even Close!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: June is shaping up to be one of the biggest months in AI, especially in 2026. We are expecting top models coming from OpenAI and Anthropic next week.
items.transcriptSummary: June is shaping up to be one of the biggest months in AI, especially in 2026. We are expecting top models coming from OpenAI and Anthropic next week.

transcript.text: June is shaping up to be one of the biggest months in AI, especially in 2026. We are expecting top models coming from OpenAI and Anthropic next week. From Anthropic, [music] we're expecting a new Mythos model, which I'll get into in a bit. But from OpenAI, we are expecting GPT-5.6. [music] And based off early sources, GPT-5.6 is going to be built off of the Kindle Alpha checkpoint. I'll show you guys some generations from Kindle Alpha to show you guys how capable these models have gotten, and why next week is probably going to be one of the biggest weeks in AI. First of all, GPT-5.6 is going to address one of the key gaps that OpenAI has at the moment, which is front-end generation. The models are really good when it comes to thinking and reasoning at the moment, but the area it struggles in is front-end generation. And that area is something that is noticeable for a lot of users, and it feels like on purpose OpenAI hasn't prioritized that, but they realized in order to win back more market share, especially from Anthropic, they need to upgrade their front-end generation. So we can see that the front-end generation apparently has improved significantly, producing much stronger outputs without requiring heavy prompting or skills. So if the model is able to improve its front-end generation without requiring heavy prompting, that means even at the base level, the model is much more capable when it comes to UI generation or any front-end related task. As well, the model has strong vision capabilities, and it performs well on image understanding and image tasks. At the moment, the top model when it comes to image generation is GPT image 2. So this is not a big surprise to see that GPT-5.6 is able to do that as well. The training is probably shared across both platforms, and being able to continue performing well on image understanding and image reference tasks, it's really important because it helps you number one, continue improving GPT image, and number two, if you guys use Codex, you're probably familiar with this. The models, the way it tests itself as well debugs itself, is by literally taking a screenshot of its generation and then feeding it back to the model and working on top of that. So, having stronger image understanding as well image reference tasks is going to be really essential not only just for the model as a base level or GPT image, but also for Codex related tasks. And we also know that GPT 5.6 is going to improve on overall output quality. And according to a lot of people, it appears noticeably better with improvements across reasoning, coding, and UI generation. So, I already talked about UI generation, but reasoning and coding is obviously essential for OpenAI. They are trying to build Codex into a super app, and in order for that super app to really exist and be attractive to a lot of developers as overall consumers, the models that power Codex have to have strong reasoning and coding capabilities. So, if GPT 5.6 is actually able to deliver what we're seeing on screen, this is going to be a big jump for OpenAI as well because we know that Anthropic is not slowing down either. Now, I'm going to show you guys a couple of generations from GPT 5.6. First up, let me show you guys two variants of GPT 5.6 that were being tested. So, the first one on top is Kepler Alpha extreme high, and at the bottom is Kindle Alpha. And as I mentioned, GPT 5.6 is probably going to ship with Kindle Alpha based off the early leaks. But what we can tell is that both of these checkpoints, number one, it's clearly evident that Kindle Alpha is stronger performing. This image might be hard to see for you guys, but if you look at it closely, what you can see is that Kepler Alpha is a little bit blocky when it comes to voxel art. It is supposed to be on par to Kindle Alpha, but it looks like Kindle Alpha has a slight edge. I'm just basing this off of these two generations that I'm seeing. But what's really important to know is that this prompt was ran on both models on extreme high and you can compare these outputs yourself to see if you guys really think that GPT 5.6 is going to be a big jump compared to GPT 5.5. Now, this is just one generation that we're seeing, but as I said, this model is going to be competing with mythos. And according to this individual who also has tested mythos and other people in the space, they are saying that mythos is probably going to overtake GPT 5.6. The models are not going to be able to compete with what Anthropic is putting out. Obviously, we don't know if that's going to be the case, but these are just early speculations. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. This is another example of GPT 5.6. This is a zero-shot SVG output from 5.6 with a one-sentence prompt and no tools enabled. And in the person actually gave it an image of Xbox One controller. So, the model had to recreate the Xbox controller with a reference image and we can see that it's pretty coherent. Like this looks pretty close to an Xbox One controller, but as I said, this was given with a reference image. When the model is not given any reference image, we can see something like this at the bottom over here where it's okay. It's not like, you know, the best, but also this was at medium reasoning effort. So, this is a good improvement, but obviously it's not like a crazy jump that we think of, right? This is once again the Kinder Alpha checkpoint at medium reasoning effort. But as I said, the biggest improvement that we are probably going to see is UI improvement. So, we can see over here that this is a website that I had created and this was actually created with no skills to use and it was straight out of API. And one more point which was that the prompt was also short. It was not a lengthy one. So, this is a big jump from what we know GPT 5.5 is capable of. Obviously, this is not like a crazy UI generation, but if you have been working with GPT 5.5, you know that the front end is not the best. And if you're able to create this with a simple, you know, one sentence or a small sentence prompt, then you might be happy using GPT 5.6. You know what I mean? So, keep that in mind. And to make things more interesting, Anthropic is not dropping Mythos 1 preview as we originally expected. Based off early leaks and a model slug appearing, it looks like Anthropic might be shipping Claude Mythos 5 model instead of, you know, Mythos preview 1, for example. And Claude Mythos, this was kind of expected by all of us, is going to be released as its own model class besides Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus. So, maybe if you guys are using Opus, you might not be using it for a long time anymore. Most people might switch to the Mythos model, especially Mythos 5. Obviously, this is only going to be the case if the model is able to be cheap enough to run for most people. I don't think, at least at the moment, that is going to be the case. It's 3.2 times more expensive than Opus based on early leaks, and it could be actually pretty hard for a lot of people to use this model, especially given rate limits and everything like that. But, this is going to be a completely different model class, and this is going to be released as a Mythos 5 completely. Why would that be the case? Because Mythos has been already in deployment for about 4 months now. So, obviously, as this person says, the one that we are going to get is probably going to be a weaker variant of the model because of how strong this model is and how concerned Anthropic is releasing it to the general public. So, we are going to get probably a distilled version of Mythos. That's my understanding, at least at the moment. And some people are saying that this is probably going to be the case because of IPO. We know Anthropic filed for an IPO. So, if they're able to drop a model that, you know, attracts a lot of people back to Anthropic, especially with the new model that they're about to drop, that is going to be a big win for them. Now, everyone is not super excited about this model dropping cuz as this person is saying that he had early access to this model and in his limited testing, the model can automate an entire software company. So, that's going to cause a lot of real business disruption for a lot of people. Explain how to take over the world. This, I think, is a little bit fear-mongering. I don't know if, you know, that's really going to affect how people think about what this model does. Reason about an existential risk for 16 hours. Design a new programming language, which I think is a unique capability of a model. That'd be kind of interesting to see if people are able to create their own programming languages just for themselves. And then threaten the entire labor market. I think this is something that we expect with AI anyways nowadays. We know when a new model comes, it's going to be stronger and it's going to cause a lot of disruption. But based off this person, the model is so strong that he is worried that it's going to, you know, cause a big change for a lot of people, especially for software companies and especially for the labor market, which I think are reasonable concerns. But as I said, it might be a little bit fear-mongering because I don't know if that is really going to be the case. Now, Metis 5 is once again a very capable model just like GPT-5.6, but maybe a little bit better because look at this. It took about 8 minutes and 25 seconds to make this SVG code at the bottom over here to create a Nintendo Switch. And this person is literally feeling ecstatic while posting this. So, obviously this person is really into the AI space, so I can understand why. But this generation looks very real. This looks more like an image compared to, you know, previous SVG code generations we have seen, especially the screen part. The controllers look a little bit blocky and you can tell that this is not a picture, but this part looks very realistic. And just to continue, after the Nintendo Switch, they made a PSP SVG code and once again as I said, this looks like an image. Like look at the middle part over here, which I think is a crazy jump for a model. Obviously, there are some glitches at the side over here, but this part tells me that this model is going to be very capable and this took about 10 minutes and 46 seconds to create this. Now, what's really interesting over here is that this person is saying that this is Mythos preview, not Oceanis. Mythos 5 is nerfed. So, as I expected, the model we actually get in production is not going to be as capable as what we're seeing right now. That is going to be the case and we know that Google has been, you know, doing that for a while as well and I think Anthropic might be doing that because number one, rate limits and number two, the cost associated with the model. It's not going to be able to, you know, be adopted very easily by a lot of people. If if the cost is so high that it outweighs the benefits, you know, what I mean? So, that's why you might get a nerfed version of this model. And just to show you guys more generations, this is a PS4 and a PS5 SVG with detailed prompts. This person is being more specific about what the SVG code looks like, but if you look at the details, you can zoom in and it looks pretty realistic. So, yeah, as I said, this model is going to be strong. Now, if you have been using Anthropic's model in Notion, Notion just made a public release that they're saying that the model is experiencing degraded performance, which if you guys know and are pretty tapped into this space, this is a pattern basically that we're all familiar with now.
