Mythos preview pick the better step 64% of the time up from 22% in 2024. So, are we actually looking at recursive self-improvement? Here's where Anthropic is more careful. They say none of this guarantees it. It's still not clear Claude can do research judgment, choosing which problems are even worth working on. That taste is the gap between today and a system that could design its own successor. But, if the trends are holding the way they are, they say AI building its own successor is very plausible and they're blunt about both sides. It could transform medicine and the economy for the better or compound alignment problems and lead to loss of control. Here's where I keep landing on. The one thing standing between today and a system that improves itself is judgment. And judgment is the exact capability their own numbers say is starting to move from 22% to 64%. So, yes, AI is entering a really exciting phase and individuals like myself and you guys watching this video are obviously monitoring the space very closely and obviously understand the implications of it. But, what's really crazy is that we are still just at the beginning. But, that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel, follow our new newsletter as well at universityofai.beehive.com, as well subscribe to the main channel World of AI, and support us on X by following the University of AI Z as well. Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: OpenAI Changed Codex, Microsoft Drops 7 NEW Models & Anthropic's IPO Is Coming!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers.
items.transcriptSummary: Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers.

transcript.text: There's a new AI lab in town. Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers. So, let's get into it. A few weeks ago, Microsoft told all its employees to stop using cloud models and now we know why because Microsoft just revealed Microsoft AI today and dropped seven new models. Yes, seven new models developed in-house spanning from image, voice, transcription, coding, and reasoning forming the MAI model family, a multimodal ecosystem. So, the first model is MAI thinking one, which is their flagship reasoning, a medium-size model that matches leading models on key software engineering benchmarks and reaches human preference parity with Sonic 4.6 in a blind side-by-side evaluation and is trained from scratch on clean data without distillation, that's their claim. Then there's MAI Code 1 Flash, which is an inference efficient agentic coding model deeply integrated into GitHub Copilot, VS Code, and the Microsoft stack. At 5 billion parameters, it's comparable to Haiku, but cheaper. Then there's MAI Image 2.5 plus a flash variant, which handles both text-to-image and image editing surpassing the arena score of Nano Banana Pro already. Then there's MAI Transcribe 1.5, which they claim is a best-in-world transcription with state-of-the-art accuracy, five times faster than competitors, with domain-specific terminology support across 43 languages. Then there's MAI Voice 2, which is a natural speech generation across 15 languages able to adapt to a voice from a short sample with a lower cost flash variant coming soon as well. Now, when I first saw this, I was thinking that which company is going to be genuinely affected by this and one answer came to my mind that I think a lot of people are going to agree with is Google. Google's whole strategy has been to release a model and then leverage their distribution platform to serve it to an audience. Obviously, they could get away with a sub par model because their distribution for print is so large. Now, a player like Microsoft entering kind of puts pressure on Google. Compared to Open AI and Anthropic, Microsoft has a really strong distribution. As you can see already, these models are deeply integrated into things like GitHub, VS Code, the whole Microsoft stack. So, a lot of people are already going to start working with these models in the background. That has been the whole play for Google and Microsoft, and that is why I think this is going to shake up the whole competitive environment, especially for players like Google. Obviously, these models have just launched, so it's early to tell how competitive they're going to be, but based on what they are saying, they're competitive to Sonic 4.6. So, they're not competing with the latest models at the moment, but this is just a company that was launched a couple of days ago. So, we are going to see more development from Microsoft, and I'm just excited because more competition always means more innovation and more pressure to produce the best quality models. Today was probably one of the most important days for Open AI as a business, but a lot of people are going to miss that because as developers or as people who watch this channel, we're so focused on the consumer side of things. But today, Open AI had a conference called Intelligence at Work, and their pitch was basically that more than 5 million people now use Codex every single week. Originally, Codex started as a tool for software development, but now it's becoming increasingly useful for more kind of work, work that developers are not doing. Think about analysts, marketers, operators, designers. And what's really cool to know is that they make up about 20% of overall Codex users at the moment, but this segment is growing more than three times fast as the developer segment, which kind of shows you how the Codex platform is going to transition into something that is going to be used not only for coding or programming, but going to be used in actual enterprise work. Why is that important for OpenAI as a business? Developers definitely make up majority of the platform at the moment, 80% as they mentioned. But they are not going to be the ones that bring majority of the revenue in the long term. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. If OpenAI wants to continue scaling and continue training models, they need to generate a lot of revenue. Majority of that revenue is going to come from enterprise contracts. Think about this, if you're a small business or even a large enterprise, if you have hundreds of employees, if you sign a contract with OpenAI, majority of the times the pricing works like this. You have a standard fee plus a revenue portion that is tied to the number employees that actually use this. So you can see why this is going to be very important for OpenAI as a business. Now what does this mean for OpenAI? Number one, more and more enterprises and employees are going to get used to their platform. Just imagine, you have somebody who spends 8 to 10 hours working on a specific platform like Codex, and then when he goes home and he does his own personal work, what platform do you think he's going to gravitate towards? Obviously OpenAI's Codex platform. So there's one side of the strategy that most people will miss. Second, as I already mentioned, revenue. Why is that important? I'll get into this later on, but Anthropic just filed for the IPO. What does that mean? The financials and balance sheets are going to become public. If OpenAI wants to file for an IPO, they need to show real revenue on their sheet for their valuation not to plummet. And if they're able to sign a bunch of enterprise contracts, get access to American SMEs, and overall expand internationally, that gives them solid runway not only as a business, but as well as a public company. Now what's really cool is that with this feature there are a couple of important things that I Number one, they launched something called sites. Sites are basically a new kind of canvas for employees ideas. Codex can take your ideas, analysis, and plans and then turn them into dashboards, planners, review workspace, project boards, whatever you want, and you can share that with your employees. As well, there's permission access, so obviously if there's a dashboard that is maybe limited to certain people on your team, unwanted employees won't be able to access it. This is a tool that might not seem big to developers common developers, but this is really important for real work. A lot of times people are creating dashboards, Power BI dashboards, things like that to analyze data, and then share those insights with their respective teams. By making that process much easier and accessible for employees and enterprises, they're going to gravitate towards a tool like that. Second is plugins. Now, there are actually six type of plugins, and they're all targeted to specific type of work. There's a data analytics plugin that helps analysts and business team answer questions with data, and they can explore product and business data, explain why key metrics change, and create reports and dashboard using tools like Snowflake, Databricks, Genie, Hex, and Tableau. There's also a creative production plugin, which helps marketing creative teams turn a brief into assets they can review. Teams can create campaign boards, make and refine display ad variations, and produce product lifestyle shots or e-commerce ready image sets with tools like Figma, Canva, Shutterstock, and more. There's a sales plugin, which helps sales team bring customer contacts into the work that moves deals forward. Sales teams can find high priority accounts and signals, prepare for customer meetings, complete follow-ups, and more. There's a product design plugin, which is built for turning early ideas into prototype teams can review. Teams can explore product directories, audit user flows, things like that. And once again, tools like Figma and Canva will power that. There's a public equity investing plugin that helps investors make sense of market and company information. They can review earnings, compare companies, track signals, and they're going to be using tools like Moody's DataLoop, DataSite, FactSet, and more. And then there's an investment banking plugin, which can help bankers turn research and diligence into client-ready materials. They can prepare pitch materials, analyze comparable companies and transactions, and more. So, you can see OpenAI is heavily targeting six area: data analytics, creative production, product design, sales, and public equity investing, which pretty much cover majority of the corporate environment. You might have very niche stuff that might not be highlighted in the plugins directly, but these plugins probably give you a good head start to start creating and customizing your own plugins. But, what's really important is that we are now entering phase two of AI. Phase one was all about disrupting computer science, software engineering, that type of pipeline. Now, we're entering more into knowledge work area, and this phase two is going to grow pretty fast, as I said, because right now it's going three times faster than the developer community. So, right now Codex might be about 20% of investors and bankers. Maybe in the future we're going to see a split that might surprise a lot of people. And like I mentioned, on June 1st, which was Monday of this week, Anthropic has filed for an IPO. Obviously, this is still the beginning of the pipeline, but what this is going to do is overall give us a better idea of how profitable and how strong these companies are actually are. Obviously, the valuation numbers that we see right now are crazy to see. They're five times the biggest companies in the world right now. Think of Microsoft, these companies are bigger than them. And obviously, because the valuation is taken into account the future growth and projection of these companies. But, when these companies become public, especially Anthropic and OpenAI, we'll get more insight into their actual cost, their revenue structure, what their balance sheet looks like, and that'll give us a better understanding of what their actual valuation is, especially as people keep on using the term AI bubble. By seeing these companies go public, we'll get a better understanding if we are actually in AI bubble, or this is just the beginning of something big that's coming very soon. But that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel, follow our new newsletter as well at universeofai.beehive.com, as well subscribe to the main channel World of AI, and support us on X by following the universeofai.z as well. >> [music] >> Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: MiniMax M3: Rivals Opus, 17x Cheaper, Fully Open!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal.
items.transcriptSummary: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal.

transcript.text: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal. And what's really surprising is the price. You're getting state-of-the-art performance, open weights, and it's significantly cheaper than most of the frontier models people use every single day. On a few benchmark, it's actually ahead of Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5, which to be honest, I did not expect from an open model. I'll also get into the demos I ran, but first, let me show you guys what's actually powering this model, because the architecture over here is doing a lot of the work. So, let's get into it. A huge part of this actually comes from MiniMax's sparse attention, which is their new MSA architecture. This is what actually enables the 1 million token context window on M3, with a guaranteed minimum of 512,000. The reason that matters is the way it handles long context. Standard attention has every token look at every other token, so the cost climbs up fast, and the model slows down on big inputs. MSA is sparse. It scores blocks of the content, picks the ones that matter, and only runs attention over those. So, you keep the long window without the speed falling off. That's what makes it strong for long horizon agents, large coding projects, and understanding lengthy videos and documents that most people are doing nowadays. MiniMax M3 is also natively a multimodal model. A lot of models add vision by training a separate image encoder and stitching it onto a finished text model. However, MiniMax trained text and images together from the start, scaling pre-training past 100 trillion tokens, so the image and text representations live in the same space instead of being bridged in at the end. And the reason that shows up in real use is that the model can reason over an image in the same pass as the text around it. In their form filling test, it put every field onto a blank form image at the right coordinates, with the right spacing, and it worked through coordinate math before writing anything. So, for stuff like parsing documents, reading screenshots, or anything layout heavy, it's acting on the structure of the image rather than just describing it. Now, if we look at the benchmarks, specifically the coding and agenting benchmarks, it's strong across the board. On the software engineering bench pro, it sits at 59%, which is past GPT 5.5 and actually Gemini 3.1 pro, and closing in on Opus. On terminal bench 2.1, it sits at 66%, MCP Atlas at 74, and in browser comp at 83.5 sitting above Opus 4.7 79.3. The part that's important to me is that an open weight model is beating Opus and GPT 5.5 outright on a chunk of these. Browser comp, SVG bench, kernel bench hard, and OS world. That hasn't really been the case before. The honest take is obviously these are just benchmarks. How it actually performs in production is what matters at the end of the day. So, I would do some independent testing myself, and you should do it as well to get your own opinion on it. But, the long horizon runs are harder to cherry-pick, so let me show you guys example of what they did with that. So, there are three examples of long horizon work that they showed that are worth talking about. The first is a CUDA kernel one. They handed it empty an FP8 gem kernel to optimize on Hopper GPUs, and they actually didn't even give it working code, just a simple task description and a broken Triton skeleton. And, it worked on it for about 24 hours, ran 147 benchmark submissions, and almost 2,002 calls, and pushed the hardware utilization from 7.6% up to 71.3%, which is about a 9.4 times speed up with nobody touching it. The second is reproducing an ICLR 2025 paper from scratch, or which took about around 12 hours, 18 commits, and replicated the core experiments with the multimodal side reading the charts straight out of the paper, and the long window holding the paper, the code, and the logs all at once. And, the third, which I think is really crazy, is the post-train bench, where the model has to run its own training pipeline, synthesize data, train models, evaluate, iterate, and no humans can be involved. M3 came third on that behind only Opus 4.7 and GPT 5.5. What all three examples are really testing though is long horizon coherence, whether the model can hold its state and recover from its own mistake across thousands of steps instead of derailing, you know, 20 steps in. That's been the actual ceiling on agents being useful in production, and these runs show you that it has improved on a model you can actually host yourself. Now, pricing is the other reason that this is worth paying attention to, and MiniMax is actually sponsoring today's video, so you get an additional 12% discount, but there's a promo running at 50% off, which puts input at 30 cents per million tokens and output at $1.20 for a model going up against GPT 5.5 and Opus. That's well under what they charge. The subscription route is also their token plan, and the thing that's a bit different that there is text, image, speech, and music all drawn from one shared pool, so it covers the whole model family rather than just M3. The $20 tier gets you roughly 1.7 billion M3 tokens a month, and as I mentioned, there's a 12% discount for you guys on every tier. The link is in the description and the pinned comment, and you can actually run this with the standard API, the MiniMax code platform, or you can drop it straight into Cloud Code, Cursor, and CodeX, so wherever you use it. So, it's definitely worth trying out for yourself. So, now I'm showing you guys a couple of generations that the new model generated, and every one of these generations was built by calling the M3 API directly. It's a simple setup, point it at MiniMax's endpoint, add your key, set the model to M3, and it's OpenAI and Anthropic SDK compatible, so just swap the base URL, and I'll link the docs below just in case you guys are confused about how to do it as well. This is the glassmorphism design that I told you to create, and it created a professional dashboard. So, this is not bad at all, like this is obviously a one-shot generation, so based off the prompt I've given it and what it has created is pretty spot on. And then this is the second generation I told you to create, which was a new brutalism style, which is one of the new styles that a lot of people have been testing with the new models because number one, it has a specific style as well as very vibrant and the model has to do a good job of representing that. So, this example is kind of shows you that it's done a pretty good job. It has added animations as well as style formats and everything is coherent. And this was supposed to be for a SaaS-based company, which we can see that it's clearly doing over here. So, this is pretty good. What I really like was like for example, even at the bottom, the small details like this, a tiny CLI for tracking tasks and everything. I think that shows the personality of the new model a little bit, which I like. And then this is kind of like a portfolio page I told it to create, which I think all three of these examples show that when it comes to front-end design, it's pretty good. It's on par with many of the models. Obviously, it didn't generate any images because I didn't ask it to, just a simple template for HTML portfolio website, which is doing a good job of. And as I said, these three examples show the front-end side of the things, but the next couple of generations shows the SVG side of things. For example, this is a whole lot on SVG animation of a sunrise and sunset. So, you can see that the sun just went down, which shows you how capable this model is when it comes to SVG generation. And it has a pretty high ranking on this benchmark as well. And remember, SVG, this is all coded. This is all math and shapes and formats and everything programmed into this. So, this is why it's important to understand and appreciate that because all of this is programmed. So, even if I for example, just to show you guys, if I just minimize this, all of this will stay coherent because it's SVG code generation. Another example is this. Let me just reload this so you guys can see. I told it to create an animated SVG logo for a company called Orbit. And we see it did a pretty great job of doing that. Like this is pretty nice and everything. And as I said, SVG, so you can change this up and it'll work. There's a particle that's floating around a little bit out of place, but that's fine. I'll cut it some slack. So, this is not bad at all. And then these last two generations I'm going to show you are kind of like simulations that I told it to create. So, the first simulation that you're looking at is the solar system one and I think this kind of shows you how capable the model is. This is a 3D simulation and you can see the solar solar system. You can increase the time speed, you can zoom in. The sun looks pretty nice and everything and it's pretty coherent and if we look at the different planets, it did a good job of representing them as well. And what's really cool is as I said, you can change the time speed over here. So, we can slow it down, things like that, increase it. So, this is at 0.5 times the speed. So, this shows you how capable the model is cuz all of this is one-shot coded, right? So, if you were to iterate with the model, you can get more powerful, you know, designs and features added on. But, this is how capable the model is when it comes to coding. Just to show you guys another example, this is a cloth simulator, which is once again hard to create, but the model is able to do that. And we can see we can interact with this. So, if I interact with it, like I can shake the cloth, everything over here. I can increase the gravity or decrease it. So, if I decrease it, obviously he's going to start levitating. versus when I increase it, it drops down. Then there's stiffness on how stiff the cloth is. Like if I set it to like one, it's going to be pretty stiff versus if I set it to zero, it can move very easily. And then there's the wind feature. So, right now I've increased the wind and you can see that it's being blown and everything like that. If I decrease it, it drops down. So, this is like one of the other things that you can see. Like this is one-shotted a cloth simulator and it shows you how powerful the model is. So, based off the initial tests I've done, I can clearly tell that this model is definitely an improvement from M2.7 and is a great model for people to use based off the price point and based off its capabilities. You're going to get similar performance to the frontier models at a fraction of the cost. So, if you're thinking about switching over or if you're thinking about trying out a new model or a new subscription plan, MiniMax M3 might be the play for you. I don't know. You guys let me know what you guys think if you have tried this model out, but I think it's definitely a model to have in your back pocket and try out for a couple of things and then make your decision based on what you think. So, that's MiniMax M3. Quick heads up, the open weights on the full tech report drop in about 10 days. So, check back then if you want to self-host or dig into the MSA internal documents. If you want to use it now, the token plan covers the whole model family from one token pool with around 1.7 billion M3 tokens a month on the $20 tier. And there's a 12% discount for you guys on every tier. The link is in the description and it'll be pinned in the comments. So, make sure you guys check that out as well. And thank you once again for MiniMax for sponsoring today's video. But, that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel. Follow our new newsletter as well at universeofai.beehive.com. As well, subscribe to the main channel World of AI and support us on X by following the universeofai.z as well. >> [music] >> Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: GPT 5.6 is Coming for Mythos (And So is China)!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: China is producing their own mythos level model and Google might be in trouble. AI is moving very fast and we can see exactly why.
items.transcriptSummary: China is producing their own mythos level model and Google might be in trouble. AI is moving very fast and we can see exactly why.

transcript.text: GPT 5.6 might be coming very soon. China is producing their own mythos level model and Google might be in trouble. So, let's get into it. AI is moving very fast and we can see exactly why. Last week we got Claude Opus 4.8 from Anthropic. After a few couple of weeks of low performance from Opus 4.7 and people being disappointed and people transitioning towards OpenAI because of the GPT 5.5 model, Anthropic decided to rush a release and drop Opus 4.8. Yes, the model was better than Opus 4.7 and on some benchmarks it looked better than GPT 5.5. But, what's really interesting is that benchmarks don't always translate into actual production and when people actually use the model, that's when you actually start to see how people are reacting to them. And as given, even in their blog post, they said that this was a moderate jump from Opus 4.7 and that they're saving a big jump for mythos preview, which is probably going to be coming in June. Now, AI labs are not going to let any model take the lead because the space has become very competitive and especially between OpenAI and Anthropic. Now, OpenAI is already developing a new model that is going to be GPT 5.6 and based on leaks and based on what we have seen, it looks like this model might be coming very soon. Currently, GPT 5.6 on Polymarket, for example, is set towards to be dropped on June 9th to 11th, which is about 2 weeks from now. And GPT 5.6, according to this leaker, is saying that it's actively improving and that they're saying that there's a significantly better checkpoint becoming available internally recently. So, if you guys are not aware how model testing is done, they have multiple checkpoints, they have multiple variants of a model and when one variant or one checkpoint is performing better, then they know that, okay, this is probably the version they would like to ship and that's what's happening right now. Even on platforms like EleutherAI Arena, labs drop a variant of their model, let people test it, and based on how it's performing, either fine-tune that variant or ship that variant specifically. And internally, it seems that GPT-5.6 is appearing to be better on reasoning, stronger agentic workflows, and improved front-end generation, and a major focus to be token efficient. Now, the first two are obviously great signs. We want a model that's going to be better at reasoning because that helps you produce higher quality work, and agentic workflows is becoming essential now. Every single lab has shipped a platform like Codex or Cloud Code, and they need to have agents that actually are good at agentic workflows. Obviously, your model needs to be the thinking or the brain power behind those agents, so having a model that's going to be better at agentic workflows is necessary now. Then, when it comes to front-end generation, this is a part that everyone is aware of. Even Sam Altman has mentioned this publicly that his models at the moment are not the best when it comes to front-end generation. They're the best when it comes to, obviously, thinking or reasoning. But, the caveat to that is a lot of people use models, a lot of people use LLMs to do front-end generation work. Think about websites, think about even creating applications, even on the Codex platform. So, you need your model to have better front-end generation. So, they are trying to address this with GPT-5.6. Another thing is token efficiency. Compute has become a big layer of constraint for a lot of labs. If you look at Anthropic, for example, the reason why their models were not by everyone, especially with Opus 4.7 and everything before that, is because they had a big constraint when it came to compute. You can have the most powerful model, but if people are not able to use it, or people are not able to actually use it in production, and they're hitting rate limits, as well as spending a lot to run your models, it's not going to be a model that's going to be adopted everywhere. That is why token efficiency and the architecture behind how your LLM thinks is becoming a key importance for a lot of players. DeepSeek is a lab that kind of makes money on this because they produce strong models, but their architecture is so efficient that they're able to cut costs significantly. So, GPT-5.6 is trying to address that same constraint. Obviously, OpenAI has a little bit more room when it comes to Anthropic, but that room is going to become narrow. As we all know, Anthropic has just partnered with SpaceX, which then gives them access to more compute. So, that is why GPT-5.6 is probably going to focus on token efficiency as well. Now, these are some early rumors, so everything you hear right now, obviously, we don't know what's actually going to ship, but current signals are pointing towards GPT-5.6 and GPT-5.6 Pro arriving together. Now, that does make sense given how strong the models that Anthropic is planning on releasing in June, Mita's preview, which they're hyping up to be the best model in the world, the strongest model, a model that they had to put behind closed doors because how strong it was. So, GPT-5.6 Pro arriving at the same time might make sense. Another reason why that might make sense, if you guys know, Google is dropping their new model, Gemini 3.5 Pro, in June as well. So, that is why seeing GPT-5.6 Pro arrive at the same time makes sense because of the competition in the space. But, as I said, all of this is early rumors, and as I get more information, I'll keep you guys updated. And when these models do drop, make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel because I'll be giving you guys updates as soon as they come and test it out for you. >> Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. >> Now, this is the first time I'm hearing about this, but I wanted to make sure that you guys are also updated, is that there are some rumors that there's a Chinese model that is going to be coming soon that is going to surprise everyone because it could be beyond Mita's level. Now, that is a big claim. Beyond Mita's level means that this is going to be better than GPT 5.5 currently as well Opus 4.8, which I don't think is going to be the case. I think most likely it will be competitive like MeToo's level, but obviously we don't know how competitive MeToo's is going to be until it actually ships. But, this makes sense because right now everyone has been focused on the Western labs with OpenAI and Anthropic. The race between them is very competitive as well Gemini is trying to be competitive again dropping Gemini 3.5 Pro in June. So, we will see how competitive Gemini becomes because they have currently lost the lead a little bit, but the Chinese labs are obviously not going to slow down. So, if Anthropic is planning on releasing a MeToo's level model, we are going to see variants of that through the Chinese labs as well. It could be DeepSeek, it could be Kimmy, it could be MiniMax. We don't know which lab is actually going to ship this, but we do know for sure that number one there's going to be a model that will be almost on par as MeToo's because I don't know if they have the compute or the training infrastructure to surpass MeToo's at least at the moment. But, what we do know for sure is that China is going to produce a model as capable as MeToo's at a substantially lower cost. And hopefully maybe it might be an open-source variant of it. Anthropic made this model ahead of 4.7, obviously, on every test in their table and ahead of GPT 5.5 basically across the board except for one, which is agentic terminal coding. Models jump to conclusions. They say a task is done when it actually isn't or claim a fix worked when the bug is still there. Anthropic says 4.8 is better at not doing that. The specific claim by Anthropic is that under evaluations, 4.8 is about four times less likely than 4.7 to let flaws in its own code go by without flagging them. Obviously, that's a company self-report, so weigh that accordingly to your own experience, but the honesty in code claim is something that we will all be testing over the next couple of weeks. Now, the biggest technical piece, a new Claude code feature called dynamic workflows, is in research preview. The way it works is like this. Normally, an agent does one pass at your problem. With dynamic workflows, you give it a big task and Claude writes his own orchestration script, breaks the task into subtasks, and runs those across tens to hundreds of subagents at the same time. The part that makes it more than just parallel work is the checking feature that they added. Some agents attempt the problem from independent angles, other agents try to refute what those found. It keeps on looping until the answer converges, then verifies the output before it comes back to you. The coordination happens outside the conversation, so the plan doesn't drift as the task gets bigger. And if a run is interrupted, it resumes instead of restarting. Fast mode for 4.8 is three times cheaper than it was on previous models, $10 per million input tokens, and 50 for output. So, keep that price in mind when you're using it. They're actually holding back, and we know why they're holding back. Now, a lot of people now, when they hear Mythos, the first sign that goes off in their head is that this is a marketing model. This is a marketing model. That could be the case. So this model is definitely beating all the other Chinese labs at the moment. But now the real question is how does this actually stack up against the newest models from the western labs? Because beating Deepseek is one thing but beating Opus 4.7 and GPT 5.5 is a whole different conversation. On the artificial analysis intelligence index which is the standard composite score people use GPD 5.5 is leading with a 60.3 max and cloud opus 4.7 both tie and score 57. So on a raw intelligence, open AAI is still at the top while Quen is tied with Opus, which itself is a really good accomplishment for Alibaba. Then it gets more interesting on the coding side. On Terminal Bench 2.0, Quen 3.7 Max scores 69.7 against Opus 4.6 Max at 65.4 on software engineering Bench Pro and MCP Atlas. Quinn also leads Opus. The newer Opus 4.7 is stronger than 4.6, six, but the agentic coding gap is still meaningful and Quinn is clearly the one ahead on all those specific evaluations. Then there's Gemini 3.5 Flash, and this is where the pricing conversation matters. On Google's published benchmark table, Flash leads MCP Atlas at 83.6 and Tulathon at 56.5. Opus 4.7 less software engineering bench pro at 64.3. GPT 5.5 less terminal bench 2.1 at 78.2. So Flash, which is a cheap mid-tier Google model, is already running ahead of the flagships on two use benchmarks. Pro hasn't even shipped yet, which is coming next month. So where does that leave Quen 3.7 Max? It's API only, $2.50 per million input tokens, $7.50 per million output. For comparison, GPT 5.5 is $5 per input and $30 per output. So Quentyn is roughly half the price on input and one quarter on output. And it natively supports the enthropic API protocol. You can point cloud code, open claw or any enthropic compatible harness directly at the quen endpoint as a drop in. So if you're building agents, you can literally swap your endpoint tonight and try for yourself. Now the catch, the model is verbose. Artificial analysis observed roughly 97 million tokens generated during their evaluation, which is far above the median of 24 million. Per token prices add up faster than they look on paper, especially on long agentic runs. But that's your trade-off. But to sum up, I'm not saying Quinn has overtaken GBT 5.5 or Opus 4.7 on general intelligence and writing quality. Open AI is obviously still ahead. Enthropic is still the model most developers trust for production. But 6 months ago, the conversation was American Frontier, Chinese openweight, and deepseek as a cheap option. That framing is basically breaking now. From what's currently happening with Quen's release cadence and the gap they just opened on Long Horizon Agentic Work, this is usually a clear signal that a lab is hitting a real groove and I think Quen ends up being the third name in the Frontier conversation by end of the year and Deepseek ends up being the one trying to catch back up. Anyway, that's the model I think most people are sleeping on right now. So, it's worth keeping an eye out on Alibaba for sure. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. There are also some early leaks at a mode coming to Gemini 3.5 Pro, which we know is coming next month. You usually only see that kind of error when a parameter exists in the API but isn't switched on yet. So, Google is appearing to be getting prepared for an extreme high thinking tier and it lines up with the 3.5 Pro launch window next month. For context, the current Gemini 3 Pro thinking level parameter accepts low or high, and Gemini 3.1 Pro added a medium tier. No Gemini model has had an extreme high setting yet. Meanwhile, OpenAI shipped GPT 5.5 with five effort levels, including extreme high, and that's the one that's leading the leaderboard at the moment. Right now on the artificial analysis intelligence index, GPT 5.5 extreme high leads at 60. GPT 5.5 high is second at 59. Then cloud opus 4.7 Max and Gemini 3.1 Pro preview are tied at 57. The leak itself is just an API string. We don't know how much extreme high actually adds for Gemini. For reference on OpenAI side, the jump from high to extreme high on GPT 5.5 is one point on the index. So, it's not a big jump on its own, but enough to retake the leaderboard if we're already close. And Gemini Pro is already tied with Opus, right behind GPT 5.5. The other thing worth keeping in mind is the Agentic side. Gemini 3.5 Flash already posted big gains on Agentic Evaluations and MCP2 calls. If extreme high thinking layers on top of that for the Pro model, you end up with something that's both reasoning heavy and tool capable in the same release. I'm not too confident on exact numbers until the actual launch, but what we know currently, 3.5 Pro is going to be a big deal for Google because the 3.5 flash model release was a little bit underwhelming and people are not super impressed with it. If 3.5 Pro is able to compete with GPC 5.5 and Opus 4.7 and retake the lead, Google might be actually back then. This is the second big API price cut from a Chinese lab in less than a week. Earlier today, Xiaomi posted that they're permanently dropping prices on the Mimo version 2.5 series by up to 99% compared to previous pricing, effective tonight at 6. The actual numbers are pretty wild. MIMO version 2.5 Pro, their flagship, is now 0.0036 per million tokens on cash hit input and 0.435 on cash miss, 0.87 on output. The cash hit price is a 98 to 99% drop from where it was. And on the smaller version 2.5 model, output is now 28 cents per million tokens. Xiaomi released MIMO version 2.5 and version 2.5 Pro in late April, both under the MIT license, making it open source. The Pro is a mixture of experts model with 1.02 trillion total parameters and 42 billion active. It's trained for agentic work consisting long horizon task across more than a thousand tool calls and the benchmark numbers are competitive with top closed source models. Software Engineering Bench Pro, for example, it's at 57.2 and the claw eval sits at 63.8. And the timing here is what's interesting. Deepseek did this exact move earlier this week. They made their 75% version pro discount permanent. Input dropped to 0.435 per million tokens output to 0.87, which puts version pro at roughly 7 times cheaper than anthropic and nearly nine times cheaper than OpenAI on output tokens. items.title: The NEW Best ASR - NVIDIA Nemotron 3.5 ASR
items.author: Sam Witteveen
items.summary: Okay, so one of the things that got lost in all the Nvidia announcements last week was that they also released a new ASR or rather a streaming ASR model. So this is the Neotron 3.5 ASR model and this is basically a 600 million checkpoint that can probably replace your whole speechtoext stack in here.
items.transcriptSummary: Okay, so one of the things that got lost in all the Nvidia announcements last week was that they also released a new ASR or rather a streaming ASR model. So this is the Neotron 3.5 ASR model and this is basically a 600 million checkpoint that can probably replace your whole speechtoext stack in here.

transcript.text: Okay, so one of the things that got lost in all the Nvidia announcements last week was that they also released a new ASR or rather a streaming ASR model. So this is the Neotron 3.5 ASR model and this is basically a 600 million checkpoint that can probably replace your whole speechtoext stack in here. You can basically self-host it. You can get it to do a whole bunch of the features like streaming audio and stuff like that. In this video, I'm going to talk about the model. I'm going to talk about some of the things around it, and I'm going to go through some demos of actually how well it does for streaming audio, how you can use it to do things like word boosting to make it more accurate for your particular use case, and also how you can use it in a diorization system to be able to get speaker level attribution, etc. So, up until recently, most of the streaming ASR models that you could actually self-host, things like Whisper, etc., were great for sort of offline or batch prediction, but they weren't really any good for streaming use cases. So, what Nvidia has released here is a 600 million parameter streaming model that can transcribe 40 languages from a single checkpoint. And this is coming out of the Nemo speech team, which Nvidia has had for a while, and has really been just pretty relentless in shipping new versions. So I talked about parakeet on the channel before. That was one of their models and this has sort of benefited from all those lessons of those models coming down to the 600 million parameter checkpoint. Now I wouldn't say that it's perfect but it is pretty amazing what you can actually do with this. And for me personally just playing with it over this weekend I'm already starting to use it instead of other models that I basically had running locally. All right. So one of the key things about this model is that it's what's called caware streaming. So what does that actually mean? So what's going on here is that this basically solves the problem around sort of buffered streaming. The old way to make a non-streaming encoder work on live audio is to feed it overlapping chunks. And each time you're feeding it these overlapping chunks, you're taking a window of audio, you transcribe it, then you slide the window forward, but you overlap it. Then basically sort of end up transcribing these in between chunks or these overlap parts multiple times. And that means you end up reprocessing that overlapping audio at every step, repeating this sort of same work. And this sort of just adds to your delay. You're literally paying for that compute to re-encode the audio that you've already had encoded. So quesaware basically flips that. Instead of reprocessing the overlaps, the model ces the encoder's self attention and activations and then reuses those C states as new audio arrives. You can kind of think of this as like a KVK trick that you use in LM decoding for a streaming audio encoder. the work that you've already computed on those earlier frames is stored. So when the next chunk comes in, the model attends to the ced representations rather than having to recomputee them from the raw audio. And this basically just saves on compute and speeds up everything. Now in video reports that this can make it up to 17 times better. They're measuring it on an H100. I've been running it on the DGX. I definitely noticed that this is faster than the standard models, but my guess is that your mileage may vary depending on what kind of GPU you're running this on. And I should point out that I've been running it at full resolution, but literally just as I started to record this and look at this, people are already running quantized versions of this and I think they have MLX versions already out, which is kind of amazing how quickly the community is moving. So one of the big things that will determine the speed, especially if you're doing this for live streaming is, you know, setting the actual chunk size. So in the code they basically have you can set the attention context size in here. And this inference setting is kind of trading off latency for accuracy. You don't need to change anything about the training of the model. This is just an inference setting that you set at runtime. And you can basically set those chunk sizes to be 80 milliseconds, 160 milliseconds, 320, 560, or just over 1 second. And I'll show you in the demo where I'm doing this streaming that you certainly can see the difference between, say, the 80 millisecond transcribe chunks versus the 1 second ones. Now, for me, I don't notice a huge difference in accuracy. I'm sure it's there, and I'm sure for special use cases, you're probably going to notice it a lot more. For me though with the streaming element, it's more down to do you want every word transcribed sort of almost like word or two words at a time or do you want phrases being transcribed? And that's what sort of the 1 second one does as opposed to the shorter chunk size. All right. So you can see here in the model card the reasons why they're saying you should actually select this model. They do talk about punchualization and capitalization. To be honest, I haven't found that to be great for the streaming version of it, but I kind of feel like even the best streaming models in the world are not always great at punctuation when you're actually doing streaming. All right, so one of the big advantages of this model is the ability to have different languages. So, there are 19 languages out of the box that just work perfectly. You can even set it to sort of auto detect for those, although you will probably have some mixed results if it's streaming. But once you set them, they should work pretty well. On top of those 19, they've got 13 ones which they say are production level for this. And then finally, they've got adaption ones. So this is eight languages that the model can adapt to. So for example, Thai. I tried this one out and with their base model, it really doesn't work properly at all. You need to fine-tune it for this. So, my guess is what they're saying is that they've had pre-training on these other eight languages, but if you wanted to actually use them for anything serious, you'd want to go and fine-tune them yourself. Now, I think they either have or they're in the process of releasing some of the fine-tuning code and some examples to do that. And I am sort of curious to see myself like how much data would it actually take to fine-tune this model to be really good at TAI. and it's something that I may look at in the future. But generally the way this works is you can pass this in as a code and it's then doing conditional generation. So it's generating based on the input plus the code for the actual language to generate the output. So another feature that you can get working with this which for me is really cool is the whole idea of word boosting. So I think on some of the inference providers they're providing this already. I was able to get it working with my own scripts in here. So if you don't know what word boosting is is that you can think of all these kind of models as being biased towards what they heard most in training, right? That means like if there are certain words that are very common in English, it will default to that and that's great for your average kind of vocabulary. The problem is what if you've got things like product names or drug names or code names or you know people's surnames or even things like model names that are much rarer and probably were never in the actual training data then the model does really badly at that. So a classic example of this would be my surname. The models are not going to basically get that right. And so in the past what people would do is that they would fine-tune for this kind of thing. That's not a great solution. It often tends to be sort of expensive, slow, and it can be really sort of overkill if you just want it to know 20 different product names and then in 3 months from now those products are going to change or something. Really, what you want to be able to do is pass those in at decode time. And that's exactly what word boosting actually is. It's a decode time technique. There are no weight changes, no retraining, no fine-tuning. you're basically just handing the model a list of words plus some kind of strength and then it becomes more likely to reproduce those words as opposed to other words that sound like those words. So this can be huge if you're trying to transcribe things with product names with people's names and stuff like that. And people get really annoyed and probably rightly so if your system is constantly getting their name wrong or constantly getting something specific that they want wrong. So the model is actually using what's called a boosting tree to basically generate candidates and score them. And you can think of the word boosting is basically adding in your phrase doesn't have to be a single word. It can be a phrase in there into this tree. And as the decoder basically generates the tokens out, it sort of walks along these and if it sees that, oh, your one is possibly relevant to this and it scores highly, it's then getting a positive bias added to the score. So, it basically becomes more likely to get the highest score and then be what the model actually predicts out. Now, that's only going to happen when somebody says something that's close to that particular word or phrase. Obviously, if they're saying something totally different, it's not the model's not going to be able to work out that okay, these are the same things. Now, you have to play around a little bit with how strong do you want the boosting. If it's too high, you get false positives and stuff like that. If it's too low, it's not going to work. But generally, if we've got a list of words or phrases that they themselves are quite different, you can actually pass them in and get a strong result back. as I'll try to show you when we do this in the demo. You can do this both for the streaming predictions and the non-streaming predictions. All right, let's jump into the demo and have a look at how this model actually works. Okay, so this is actually testing the model running locally. So what I've got is I've got the model running on an Nvidia machine. I'm streaming that through the local network to my Mac where I'm actually recording this. So let's see how it goes. So you'll see that I've got a few different things in here. You can select out the language that that is going to be the right language for this. We can it can also be set to auto detect, although I find that auto detect is a bit hit and miss in here. You can then set out the latency. So we'll do some experiments with latency. And I'm just using the normal microphone that I would use. All right. So as you can see, the audio is starting to show up here. This is kind of the median latency that I've got set up. Uh, but it's able to stream it pretty good. And the accuracy of this, uh, is going to work out pretty well as as well. And I actually find for sort of general use cases so far, the accuracy on the fastest one versus the slowest one isn't that much different. Okay, so I've just stopped it there. Um, we will resh this. Let's go to the fastest one. So the fastest one obviously we're just taking smaller chunks uh of audio. Uh so we're now down to 80 millisecond chunks of audio audio or 80 millisecond chunks of audio here. Uh you should see that it's basically responding quicker because it's got more times that it's actually uh being able to process each of those 80 second mills. Um it's still working out some sort of overlap and stuff like that. It's still going to be able to do this. And as you can see, the accuracy hasn't really gone down a lot. Uh, but we've got pretty good quality now streaming transcription of pretty much anything I say. And I can sort of stop. I can, you know, uh, start. Now, the the other thing is we don't have perhaps great punctuation here. Uh, if I pause and say, okay, uh, I wonder what's going to actually happen next if I keep talking here. You can see that we're not really getting anything in great in the way of punctuation with this model. If I go all the way up to the highest accuracy, we're now over a second and you're going to see that definitely h it's going to be doing it uh in chunks now. But because those chunks are bigger, uh we're still going to have a pretty nice uh sort of transcription going on here. It's just that we're getting multiple words at the same time rather than sort of chunk by chunk where each chunk is almost one word here. Uh this is still very good for uh running through the model. Uh it's meant to be higher, you know, the highest level of accuracy. Um I would say though I don't see a huge difference in the accuracy as we go through this. Uh but the great thing is you've got the option to do this. Now I was uh testing this originally using a radio interface that went through the web and definitely there I saw that okay things would actually slow down uh with especially with this one. But you can see in this case that really that's not a problem. It's able to uh keep going quite uh a long way. I've never actually gotten to the end. So I'm curious to see like okay uh it looks like we've run we've filled out the box there. Oh no. Okay. So, it was basically just catching up. Uh, and it's still going here. Uh, what are we going to see? Uh, it is very cool. I got to say that Nvidia is actually making these things available. And you will see that certain words, things like Nvidia, it it does really well at being able to transcribe that words like Google, at Microsoft, all the common sort of uh nouns and pronouns. It will actually know them and be able to transcribe them pretty well. Where this doesn't do well is with uh words that it doesn't know very well. So for example, if I talk about Neotron, it seems to know what the Neotron uh models are, but then sometimes it will say uh transcribe them correctly. And you can see sometimes it's transcribed it with an M as opposed to with an N there. The models from Quen uh are going to be spelled wrong. So really Quen should be Q W N if we're spelling it in English. Uh but you can see that when I just say Quen, it doesn't get that right. So this is where word boosting comes in. And I I'll just point out that even if we do this with a uh you know faster, lower latency transcription in here, if I say the word Nvidia, it can probably pick that up pretty well. uh if I say the word Quen, it's not going to be able to uh get that. Um so if I say Google, Microsoft, Apple, all those big companies, it kind of knows what they are, but it doesn't know uh Quen, does it know Miniax? Maybe that's probably something that it can get. Yes. Uh, but you can see that if I say my name, for example, Sam Witterine, it's going to get the Sam right, but it's totally gotten the wrong uh it's totally got the wrong spelling there for the surname. Uh, so this is what we want to fix. And and the way we're going to fix this is we're going to use word boosting. So, this is a technique which I'll explain in a second, but you'll see that what I'll be able to do then is inject certain spellings for words that are how I want them to be spelled. And it's not a swap. It's not like I'm working out how it's saying it. I'm basically just increasing the probability of how it would actually come out. So, let's look at that. Okay. So, what I've done now is I've run a version of the script where we're actually boosting certain phrases or, you know, certain words, certain phrases. So, it doesn't have to just be a word. This could be one phrase. We're basically just boosting the probability of those tokens in that order appearing. So, you'll see that I just showed you before that things like when it was getting wrong, my name it was getting wrong, etc. If I put it on and I start talking now, we should see that it's starting to transcribe. And I say, "Hello everyone. Uh, my name is Sam Witvine and I'm going to be talking to you today about the Quen models, about the Neotron models, and about how they all work together." And you can see now it's got my name right, it's got Quen, right? It's got the Neotron models right. So, this is something that I see a lot of times people don't realize that you want to be able to do this with an ASR system. You want to be able to boost certain words and phrases so that they will appear more. And that will happen not just for the that version. If I come in here with the quick version and say right uh my name is Sam Witine and I'm going to talk to you about the Quen uh models whether we're talking about Quen 36B where we're talking about Quen I see even if I come in with the uh lowest latency one and I talk about Hello everyone my name is Sam Witterine uh and I'm going to be talking to you about the Quen models versus the Neotron models and we're going to start off talking about Quen N 27 billion parameter model. Now it seems to have missed the word uh model there. Uh but the rest of it is done pretty good. Okay, with this and you'll see this is true even for uh the highest level accuracy as well. It's just that the chunks that we're going to get there are going to be longer. So if I'm talking here and I say hello everyone my name is Sam Witine and I'm going to be talking to you about the Quen 27 billion parameter model versus the Neotron model. You can see here it basically got it right. If you uh just like all these things they're not perfect but that is basically word boosting. Uh it's something that you can do with the Neotron 3.5 ASR model. It's something that you probably want to do if you're going to put something into production. Okay. So, the other thing that I haven't shown in here is speaker diorization. And there are a number of different ways that you can do this. You can either use an external model to do this or you can actually do it with the the Nemo framework. They've got a whole section for doing it in here. For me, I couldn't get this working well on the live transcripts, but certainly for doing things like podcasts and stuff like that, it works really well. And while it'll basically just section off and return back the different speakers, you can then pretty easily just substitute out speaker one for the name of that person etc. The other thing that you can do is you can capture the embeddings of the speakers. So if you've got a little bit of the recording where you know this person you know if for example I at the start of recording I say hi my name is Sam and then you captured that and you took the embedding from that you can then assign that to the different speakers and have it be able to actually return back the speakers and possibly be able to get that going with the live transcription version as well. So, in general, I got to say this is pretty awesome how useful this model is, and it's basically quite easy for you to run it locally to be able to do different kinds of ASR tasks like I've shown in here. So, anyway, let me know in the comments what you think of this, if you've actually had a chance to try this out yet. Certainly for myself, this has been a productive use of just testing this model out and then incorporating it into some of the workflows that I've got that run on my local machines here. Anyway, as always, if you found the video useful and you're interested in videos like this, click like and let me know. If you've listened right through to the end, thank you very much for that. One of the things that I am thinking of doing is starting up a second channel to do much more sort of in-depth long perhaps not as polished and edited content going through basically the code for this kind of stuff.
